name: Usnea Beard

branches.name: Deep Read
branches.grants: A specialization branch: with time and stillness, read an object's layered history in light — not just what it is, but the order of what happened to it.
branches.cannot: Needs minutes and quiet; a hurried or hostile scene forecloses it; history older than the material itself is silent.

inventory.name: Waterskin
inventory.description: Boiled and refilled at the last clean spring. Worth more every week the river runs gray.

inventory.name: Trade Tokens
inventory.description: Stamped ceramic rounds honored in Millbrook and grudgingly beyond. The valley's compromise with currency.

inventory.name: Pale Turnip Seedling
inventory.description: A color-drained turnip seedling from the eastern irrigation arm — three mornings of gray-green water have leached it pale. Physical evidence of contamination reaching the soil.

inventory.name: Crystalline Residue Pinch
inventory.description: A fingertip's worth of crystalline powder scraped from the third interface depression at the vent aperture. Nanite-structured, pre-Transition manufacture — holds light briefly rather than reflecting it. Wrapped in a ration-paper fold. Fragile.

inventory.name: Orla's Memory Glass
inventory.description: A fogged-edge photonic disc that replays what light touched at the ford-side boulder three days ago. Shows a Radiant engineer — Fendt — deliberately pouring from a sealed tube into the Echo River ford current. Warm from handling. Fragile.

inventory.name: Trail Provisions
inventory.description: Dried apple rings, hard tack, cured river-fish rope, seed-oil in a clay bottle, and a wedge of hard yellow cheese — three days' solid traveling food. From Josset's stall, three ceramic rounds.

inventory.name: Upper Zone Ridge Map
inventory.description: A charcoal-on-hide copy of the new mountain pass route and approximately two hours' ridge country beyond — shows the pass entry clearly; the interior is inference. Rolled and carried with the waterskin.

deeds.description: Stilled a boiling irrigation argument at the well with a word and a baobab's patience.
Brokered a one-week irrigation truce between Holloway and Renn, reframing a personal dispute as a shared contamination problem for the village elders
Walked the full eastern irrigation arm over two days and located the contamination's entry point — a warm seep beneath an old retaining wall with geometric patterning in the silt below
Traced the six-fold pulse south to Millbrook's drinking wells — confirmed the village water supply is inside the system's reach
Asked Pell what she needed to feel safe — and listened — earning Sela's testimony for Thursday's elder meeting.
Secured the tuning-warden's agreement to testify at Thursday's elder meeting — a Heights-side witness to the six-count pulse three springs before the crisis.
Named the seep, the lattice, the six-fold count, and Aevi's assessment plainly and in sequence before Millbrook's assembled elders — no softening, no qualification.
Invited Aevi to demonstrate the six-fold count directly above the evidence — the rhythm made plain and visible to every elder in Millbrook's common house.
Presented Orla's memory glass to Millbrook's assembled elders — Fendt's deliberate pour into the Echo River played in silence before Dresh, Holloway, Renn, and the full room.
Named the full coordination chain — Fendt's pour, the vent aperture signature, and the six-count — linking all three witnesses in plain speech before Millbrook's assembled elder panel.
Called Sela into the common house at the moment her testimony could still reach the elders — trusting Pell's nod and the room's readiness.
Offered Sela a plain, unpressured invitation — her choice, no need — and she tapped the six-count into the common house floor before Millbrook's assembled elders
Held still and let Dresh speak first — the evidence and the room did the work; Dresh named the system as operating before all of Millbrook's assembled elders.

chronicle: Usnea descends to the valley floor and sets course for the Harmonic Heights lower terrace — the conduit frame and the tuning-warden's unsettled admission pulling them forward.

turns.summary: Leaving Millbrook, Aevi senses an old alignment running northeast — a possible shortcut beneath the meadow grass.
turns.narration:
You leave the common house yard behind you, heading northeast toward the Disputed Zone's near edge and the ridge country beyond. The plateau drops away to your left, the Heights terraces catch the afternoon light across the valley, and the path ahead branches twice before the treeline — the known route adds a half-day of switchbacks through old orchard country, reliable and dull. But the valley floor reads differently to you now than it did eleven days ago. The eastern egress ford is still in your joints, and the six-count pulse is a rhythm you know the way you know the weight of your own branches. The land has a grain to it. Old Roads has been singing low and steady since you stepped out of the village.

Aevi brightens at your shoulder, condensing into a loose arc of teal motes that tilts slightly northeast — not toward the known path, but toward the slope where the hill-shoulder crests above the mill weir. "There's something running below the grass," it says, very quietly. "Old drainage, maybe. Or older than that. Straight line. The grain of the place wants to go that way." It says nothing else, but the motes hold their northeast lean like a compass needle finding true.

The hillside in question is meadow-soft, the kind of ground that looks flat until you're on it and discover it has been shaped — low berms, a buried row of stones half-swallowed by turf, the faint print of a road that has not been a road in a hundred years. Something lies under there. Whether it runs clean to the ridge or dead-ends in a thicket, you won't know until you try to read it.

turns.summary: Old Roads failed to lock onto the buried alignment — the meadow's layered human history blurs the deeper signal.
turns.narration:
You press your roots down into the meadow and open to Old Roads — the Straight Feeling, that patient hum of buried stone and compressed intention. For a moment you catch it: a faint grain running northeast, something old beneath the turf that wants to be a line. Then the valley floor swallows it. The meadow here is a palimpsest — irrigation cuts from three generations of farming, the mill weir's runoff spreading laterally through the subsoil, half a century of seasonal flooding redistributing everything. The land is too thoroughly lived-in, too much itself in the present tense, to yield its deeper grammar to a brief asking. The alignment is there — you don't doubt it — but the meadow's accumulated concreteness blurs it like fog on glass. Aevi dims briefly, a handful of motes flickering in and out of visibility against the afternoon light, which out here is simply the sun doing its plain work.

"The signal is real," Aevi says quietly, from just behind your left shoulder. "But the ground has been speaking other languages for a long time. You would need to hold the question longer than the moment will allow."

The half-buried stone berms run in an older direction than any current field boundary — northeast-ish, close enough — but whether they follow the alignment or merely approximate it, you cannot say. The mill weir glints behind you. Somewhere ahead the treeline thickens, and beyond that, the ridge country where the Disputed Zone begins to assert itself. You have your map, your legs, and the knowledge that the alignment exists. The question is how to reach the ridge before the afternoon light starts to drop.

turns.summary: Usnea travels the eastern rim path and arrives at the Edge District on the Radiant Plateau, bound for Orla's patch.
turns.narration:
The afternoon carries you northeast through the orchard switchbacks and up the long gravel shoulder you know by feel now — the limestone hollow still visible as a shadow in the drainage cut as you pass its level, Aevi drifting teal above your shoulder, quiet and watchful. The buried alignment you couldn't hold in the meadow grass keeps pulling at the edge of your attention, a hum that surfaces when the soil thins and the Precursor-braided rock lies closer to the surface. You let it go. There are roads you know, and you take them.

The eastern rim path is familiar as a scar: the three braided alignments crossing underfoot at intervals, the six-count pulse steady in the stone each time, the cliff shoulder where the south road drops out of sight behind the plateau bulk. You cross each one with the particular attention of someone who has carried these facts all the way to a council table and back. Aevi brightens briefly at each crossing, dims, brightens. Says nothing.

The Edge District receives you the way it always has — light-guide streets luminous underfoot in the slant of late afternoon, photonic crystals in the towers catching and scattering the sun into clean prismatic angles. The air here is different from Millbrook's: drier, more deliberate, shaped. At this hour the district is moving toward the quieter end of its working day — optics-weavers folding their light-frames, apprentices sweeping crystal dust from the lane edges, a pair of filtration engineers conferring over a ledger at a corner post. You know the way to Orla's patch. You have walked it before.

turns.summary: The Amber Lath keeper confirms word of Millbrook's elder decision has reached the district — and Fendt's name now appears on the accountability assembly notice in red chalk.
turns.narration:
You pass Orla's patch without stopping — the amber collar still visible through the window-light, the workshop's interior lamp burning low — and take the two-lane walk north toward the Amber Lath. The light-guide streets glow pale gold underfoot, and the district has the particular quietness of a working community that has put its tools down for the evening but not yet settled into rest. A pair of apprentices cross ahead of you, heads together, carrying a long prism-blank wrapped in cloth. They don't look up.

The Amber Lath wayhouse sits on a corner where the lane narrows — its door is the same plain cedar, its window the same warm crack of light you remember from six nights ago. The keeper is where she usually is at this hour: behind the low counter, wiping a clay cup with the same deliberate unhurry. She registers your height and shape a full breath before you reach the threshold, and something in her posture adjusts — not alarm, the recalibration of someone who keeps a precise mental ledger.

'From the weaver's patch,' you say, more habit than password at this point.

She sets the cup down. 'Heard the elder meeting went through,' she says, no particular emphasis. 'Word came up the eastern path this morning with a Heights trader — said Millbrook named a filtration engineer.' She looks at you the way she always looks at you: assessing what you need, not what you want to talk about. 'The accountability assembly notice on the district board got a second line added this afternoon. Someone wrote beneath the original in red chalk: *Fendt, south array.* It was still there an hour ago.' A beat. 'The supervisors haven't taken it down yet. That says something.'

Aevi brightens once at your shoulder — a brief teal flare — and dims again. The keeper reaches under the counter and sets out a clay cup without being asked.

turns.summary: Prism Sight confirms the keeper is sequencing her information — she has more, and is watching to see if what she offered was enough.
turns.narration:
The lamp behind the keeper burns warm and low — good light for reading, the kind that catches the small betrayals on a face without announcing itself. You let Prism Sight settle in, quiet as a slow blink, the light-history of the room layering over what you see now. Nothing theatrical. Just looking, the way a tree looks: patient, present, taking in everything without rushing it.

She holds still as she speaks — explaining the red chalk, the supervisor inaction, the accountability notice. Her face is composed and deliberate. But the light remembers a small thing: a fraction of a second where her eyes moved not to the door, not to you, but down and left — the tell of someone organizing what to lead with rather than simply answering. She isn't lying. She's sequencing. There is something she knows, and she has decided to give you part of it, and she is watching to see if the part she gave is sufficient.

Her posture doesn't change. She refills the clay cup without being asked, sets it back on the counter with the same small precision. The tell is gone almost before you catch it — a single layer beneath the calm surface, sealed back into professional stillness. Whatever she is holding, it is not for tonight unless you ask for it by name.

Aevi drifts closer to the lamp's edge, motes barely visible in the warm light, and says nothing. But the constellation tightens, just slightly.
